text: And I'd like to now present an operative video that I performed back in 2020 for a patient with a peripheral lung lesion measuring less than two centimetres that I thought was rather relevant
to the trial results.
So based on this imaging, the patient had a high index of suspicion for primary lung cancer.
And I performed an operation without a diagnosis, specifically a lingualectomy.
And here you can see I'm performing this operation minimally evasively using the robotic platform.
And we're on the left-hand side there.
I just liberated the inferior ligament and took station nine.
Over here, you can see the lymph node just above the inferior pulmonary vein being taken.
and I've taken this as a station 10 lymph node.
I'm using a pre-rolled sponge here and I'm dissecting the posterior mediastinum, identifying the subcarinal lymph node just below the left main bronchus and taking that in its entirety.
I find the dissection of the lymph nodes to be much easier using the robotic platform compared to the traditional VATS approach.
But I think both methods are superior to the traditional thoracotomy approach in terms of reducing perioperative clinical adverse events.
So after working on the posterior medial sternum, now I'm working on the artery.
Here you can see that I've delineated the vascular artery from the lingual artery and isolating the lingual artery.
I typically get around my pulmonary arteries with a vessel loop.
And here you can see I'm using a curve dissector to get around this lingular artery to isolate it and then eventually divide it.
I usually use a vessel loop for retraction and then create and widen a tunnel for the robotic stapler to go through.
So once I have created this tunnel, sometimes I would
get a stapler in there for a drive-through or drive-by before completing the stapling, just like a drive-by here, and then completing the stapling after getting the vessel around the stapler.
So lingiolectomies, I think, is one of the simpler segmentectomies to be performed.
Generally speaking, lingualectomies, trisegmentectomies and the S6 resection of both sides are considered to be simple segmentectomies and they only have one intersegmental plane to be divided.
I've taken this station 11 lymph node, which is a relatively constant lymph node here between the lingual artery and the basilar artery and working my way up.
a little bit higher for improved exposure at the time of fissure completion.
Now usually here we can complete the anterior fissure using a stapler because in this patient the fissure was fairly complete.
I'm just using my spatula here.
So sending the station 11 lymph node for frozen, making sure that's not involved and working my way up towards the anterior aspect of the mediastinum.
So just completing the anterior fissure here, like I said, with a spatula.
This patient didn't have much air leak.
If it was a little bit more of lung tissue here, I wouldn't hesitate to use an additional stapler.
Here, seeing another lymph node and removing another lymph node for analysis.
Here, once we've completed working the fissure there, we're going to retract the lung posteriorly to expose the most anterior structure, which is the pulmonary vein.
And I really want to delineate the lingular vein if possible.
So just freeing up the mediastinal tissue here and then just dissecting a little bit distally.
In this case, it's relatively straightforward to find this lingular vein.
And then what we're going to do is once again, create a tunnel for my curve dissector to get around.
And then once again, getting my vessel loop to widen the tunnel prior to stapling.
So as I was saying, the lingualectomy, one of the more simpler
segmentectomies to be performed, and I think recent studies from Japan have classified other more complex segmentectomies that involve more than one intersegmental plane to be divided.
When these are considered complex, they're also found to be associated with higher incidence of prolonged air leak.
Here you can see we've just divided the lingual vein, and so we've taken the artery,
taking the vein, so the only remaining anatomical structure is the lingular segmental airway.
And so I'm working around and just making sure that we're getting the correct anatomical delineation.
So I'm going to the Firefly view here and getting my anesthetist to park the bronchoscope in the lingular segmental airway.

And once I've confirmed that from what I believe is to be the case in the operative field, I'm going to get around the lingual airway with my curve dissector once again, and I'm going to get my vessel loop around this delineation.
And once again, to widen the tunnel to enable a stapler to be passed across.
So in this particular case, I use two 12mm ports.
So I have the option of firing from the front or from the back.
Typically for most lobectomies only use one 12mm port from the front.
So getting my robotic stapler here and dividing this
segmental airway with a green stapler.

At this stage I've only got really the fissure to complete and I think one of the really neat ways to delineate the segmental plane when you're performing a robotic operation is to use ICG which I routinely use for segmentectomies and we're going to inject ICG and as the blood flow to the lingual segment has been
has been stopped, we can see that that particular segment does not light up in green.
And so I put in a Firefly view and I mark out my staple line using my bipolar left hand.
And then what I'm going to do is I'm going to staple across using a robotic stapler, simply staple across.
to ensure that I'm following the correct intersegmental plane.
So I'm just using a series of green staplers here, and then I'm going to march across from the back to the front, making sure that I get below the divided anatomical structures.
Here you can see just using probably three or four green staplers here.
You can use black if you like.
But the 30 green has really served me well with this particular phase of the operation.
So making sure once again that I'm not including the staple structures in my staple line.
I'm going to march across from the back to the front.
And I think it's probably requiring one more staple here.
And perhaps it's just me, but generally speaking, I always use one more staple than I think I would need.
The last couple of staples in the staple line never seemed to cut properly.
So here I probably used an extra staple once again to complete this intersegmental fissure.
Once the specimen is separated from the trisegment, what I'm going to do is I'm going to put this specimen into a bag.
I typically use the anchor bag.
And in actual fact here, I'm going to complete the operation by taking the station five lymph node, even though I think the likelihood of this lymph node being involved is very low.
I still do a routine station five nodal dissection here.
as part of a routine nodal assessment.
So the lingula segment is separated.
The station 5 lymph node is taken.
I'm going to use an anchor bag, which is my preferred bag here.
They don't break in my experience.
And I'm going to instill some warm water in the thoracic cavity and sometimes do an underwater insufflation test, as I'm doing here, demonstrating there's little to no air leak.
And I've completed the resection as well as the nodal dissection.
And I sometimes apply a bit of tissue to the raw area to minimize air leak and bleeding.
